message:
fix(func,types): match C SQLite semantics for GROUP_CONCAT, substr, and NaN/Inf handling

GROUP_CONCAT was using a global separator (last-seen value) instead of the
per-row separator that C SQLite uses. SELECT group_concat(val, sep) with
varying separators now correctly produces "a+b*c" instead of "a*b*c".

substr() had multiple edge-case divergences from C SQLite. Rewrote both
the text and blob paths to match C SQLite's exact 2-phase algorithm:
phase 1 normalizes negative length, phase 2 resolves 1-based indexing,
phase 3 applies negative-length shift. Fixes start=0 quirk, negative
start+length combinations, and boundary clipping.

NaN and Infinity values from Rust's f64 parser are now consistently
rejected across all coercion paths since C SQLite's sqlite3AtoF does not
recognize "nan", "inf", or "infinity" strings:
- SqliteValue::to_integer() and to_float() filter non-finite values
- coerce_numeric() returns Integer(0) for non-finite parsed floats
- IIF truthiness check rejects non-finite values
- sign() returns Null for non-finite input
- sqlite3_column_double() C API filters non-finite values
- E2E executors (both fsqlite and sqlite) filter non-finite parsed values

Additional safety hardening:
- replace() prevents OOM from massive string expansion (>1GB limit)
- randomblob()/zeroblob() reject sizes >1GB
- printf width parsing uses saturating arithmetic with 100M cap
- percentile_disc/cont clamp NaN percentile to 0.5

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
